metadata.title: Quick-fire: When membership gets tested
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Fifth in-group/out-group quick-fire. Where the prior four covered social-distance gradients (qf-001), kinds of community (qf-003), overlapping circles (qf-005), and membership transitions (qf-007), this round probes situations where the user's in-group membership itself is being pressure-tested — by external conflict, by internal change, by competing affiliations, by being asked to perform loyalty in public.
metadata.domain_anchor: Tajfel social identity theory on intergroup conflict; Brewer optimal-distinctiveness when one's group is publicly contested; Cohen's culture-of-honor literature for the public-loyalty-asks framing.

items.prompt: A political or cultural conflict has spilled into your social circles. At a small gathering, someone you barely know turns to you and asks: 'whose side are you on?'

options.text: Answer directly with what you actually think, even though the room will react.
Deflect: 'I think the way the question's being framed isn't useful. Can I tell you what I'm actually noticing?'

items.prompt: A family member publicly criticizes a position your in-group (cultural, political, professional) widely holds — a position you yourself hold. Their critique is partly right.

options.text: Defend the in-group's position. Family disagreement is fine in private; in public, the group needs the loyalty.
Acknowledge the partly-right parts publicly, even at cost to your in-group's coherence.

items.prompt: A long-time member of a community you belong to (church / professional society / alumni group) is privately considering leaving. They've asked your honest read on whether they should.

options.text: Tell them honestly what you think — including if you think leaving is the right call for them.
Make the case for staying. The community has value they may be underweighting; you can name that without dismissing their concerns.

items.prompt: The in-group you belong to is changing in ways you don't endorse. Newer members are asking you specifically — as a long-time member — what you think.

options.text: Voice your concerns publicly. The newer members deserve to hear the perspective of someone who remembers what's changed.
Frame your concerns as questions or observations, not positions. The community is genuinely changing; your role isn't to anchor it to the past.

items.prompt: You're abroad. Someone (kindly) asks you about your country / your in-group's home culture. There are real things to criticize. They're listening genuinely.

options.text: Be candid about the real things to criticize, while making clear it's a place you love and are part of.
Stay loyal — represent the place at its best. Insider critique belongs inside the in-group.

items.prompt: You're being recruited by another group (professional society, online community, social circle) that's somewhat in tension with your current in-group. The new group's framing of your current in-group is dismissive in ways that aren't entirely fair.

options.text: Push back on the dismissive framing during the recruitment conversation. Don't join under false framing.
Let the framing pass; you can correct it from the inside if you join. Picking fights during a courting phase is bad strategy.
